1: Psalm 83 is the final Asaph psalm and the longest. אֱלֹהִים אַל-דֳּמִי-לָךְ אַל-תֶּחֱרַשׁ וְאַל-תִּשְׁקֹט אֵל (ʾĕlōhîm ʾal-dŏmî-lāk ʾal-teḥĕrash wĕʾal-tishqōṭ ʾêl, "O God, do not remain silent; do not hold your peace or be still, O God!") — three synonymous verbs of divine inaction: dāmam (to be silent, to stand still); ḥārash (to be deaf/silent — Isaiah 42:14: a woman who has been quiet in labor); shāqaṭ (to be at rest, undisturbed — the tranquility of the uninvolved). The triple negation is the urgency of the lament: three different kinds of divine non-action are all pleaded against simultaneously.
2: כִּי-הִנֵּה אוֹיְבֶיךָ יֶהֱמָיוּן (kî-hinneh ʾôyĕbêkā yehĕmāyûn, "For behold, your enemies are in an uproar!") — hāmāh: to roar, to be in tumult (used of the sea — Psalms 46:3; Psalms 65:7 — and of roaring enemy nations — Psalms 46:6). וּמְשַׂנְאֶיךָ נָשְׂאוּ רֹאשׁ (ûmĕśanʾêkā nāśĕʾû rōʾsh, "those who hate you have raised their heads!") — nāśāʾ rōʾsh: to lift the head (the gesture of arrogant self-assertion — Judges 8:28; Psalms 110:7). The enemies are described as those who hate God — their opposition to Israel is opposition to the God of Israel.
3: עַל-עַמְּךָ יַעֲרִימוּ סוֹד (ʿal-ʿammĕkā yaʿărîmû sôd, "they devise cunning plans against your people!") — ʿāram (hiphil: to be crafty, to devise schemes — the same root as the "shrewd/crafty" serpent of Genesis 3:1). Sôd: a secret council (Jeremiah 23:18 — the divine sôd in which the true prophet stands). The enemies have their own counter-sôd against God's people. וְיִתְיָעֲצוּ עַל-צְפוּנֶיךָ (wĕyiṯyāʿăṣû ʿal-ṣĕpûnêkā, "and scheme against your treasured ones!") — ṣĕpûnîm: the hidden/treasured ones (from ṣāpan: to hide, to treasure — Colossians 3:3: "your life is hidden with Christ in God").
4: אָמְרוּ לְכוּ וְנַכְחִידֵם מִגּוֹי (ʾāmĕrû lĕkû wĕnakhḥîdêm miggôy, "they say, 'Come, let us wipe them out as a nation!'") — kāḥad (hiphil: to destroy completely, to annihilate — to cause to cease to exist). The genocidal intention: to remove Israel from the category of gôy (nation). וְלֹא-יִזָּכֵר שֵׁם-יִשְׂרָאֵל עוֹד (wĕlōʾ-yizzākêr shêm-yiśrāʾêl ʿôd, "let the name of Israel be remembered no more!") — zākar (niphal: to be remembered). The goal: not merely military defeat but complete obliteration of memory — the erasure of Israel's name from human consciousness.
5: כִּי נוֹעֲצוּ לֵב יַחְדָּו (kî nôʿăṣû lêb yaḥdāw, "for they conspire together with one accord!") — yāʿaṣ: to counsel, to scheme. Yaḥdāw: together, in unified concert. What the Shema calls Israel to do toward God (to love with the whole heart), the nations are doing against him. עָלֶיךָ בְּרִית יִכְרֹתוּ (ʿālêkā bĕrît yikrōtû, "they have made a covenant against you!") — kārat bĕrît: to cut a covenant (the standard treaty formula). The nations have made a formal alliance directed against God himself — Psalms 2:2: the kings of the earth conspiring against YHWH and his Anointed.
6: אָהֳלֵי אֱדוֹם וְיִשְׁמְעֵאלִים מוֹאָב וְהַגְרִים (ʾōhŏlê ʾĕdôm wĕyishmaʿêlîm môʾāb wĕhagrîm, "the tents of Edom and the Ishmaelites, Moab and the Hagrites") — the first four of a 10-nation coalition. Edom (descended from Esau — Israel's twin, carrying the deepest fratricidal betrayal: Obad 10-14; Psalms 137:7); Ishmaelites (descendants of Ishmael — the traders who carried Joseph to Egypt — Genesis 37:25); Moab (east of the Dead Sea — descended from Lot, fighting Israel from Balak onward — Num 22-24); Hagrites (descendants of Hagar — northern Arabian tribe: 1 Chronicles 5:10,19-20). Each carries a genealogical relationship with Israel making the betrayal doubly bitter.
7: גְּבַל וְעַמּוֹן וַעֲמָלֵק פְּלֶשֶׁת עִם-יֹשְׁבֵי צוֹר (gĕbal wĕʿammôn waʿămālêq pĕleshet ʿim-yōshĕbê ṣôr, "Gebal and Ammon and Amalek, Philistia with the inhabitants of Tyre!") — Gebal (Phoenician Byblos, or Edomite Gebalene); Ammon (east of the Jordan, descended from Lot — Judg 10-11; 2 Sam 10); Amalek (the perpetual wilderness enemy — Exodus 17:8-16; Deuteronomy 25:17-19); Philistia (the coastal confederation — primary antagonist in Judges-Samuel); Tyre (the great Phoenician trading city — Ezek 26-28). The geographic sweep covers east, south, north, and west.
8: גַּם-אַשּׁוּר נִלְוָה עִמָּם (gam-ʾashshûr nilwāh ʿimmām, "Assyria too has joined them!") — lāwāh (niphal: to join oneself, to attach — the same root as Levi's name). Assyria's attachment would date the setting to the 8th-7th centuries BCE when Assyria dominated the region. הָיוּ זְרוֹעַ לִבְנֵי-לוֹט סֶלָה (hāyû zĕrôaʿ libnê-lôṭ selāh, "they lend their strength to the children of Lot!") — zĕrôaʿ: the arm/strength (Psalms 77:15; Isaiah 52:10). Assyria's massive military arm strengthening the smaller regional powers. The children of Lot (Moab and Ammon — v6) endorsed by the ancient Near East's superpower. Selāh marks completion of the 10-nation inventory.
9: עֲשֵׂה-לָהֶם כְּמִדְיָן כְּסִיסְרָא כְיָבִין בְּנַחַל קִישׁוֹן (ʿăśêh-lāhem kĕmidyān kĕsîsĕrāʾ kĕyābîn bĕnaḥal qîshôn, "Do to them as you did to Midian, as to Sisera and Jabin at the river Kishon!") — three historical precedents: Midian (the defeat of 135,000 enemy soldiers under Gideon — Judg 7-8; divine intervention that turned the enemies against each other); Sisera (the Canaanite general whose army was overwhelmed at the Kishon in the rain — Judg 4-5); Jabin (the king of Hazor behind Sisera — Judges 4:2). The Kishon — a wadi near Megiddo, Judges 5:21: "the torrent Kishon swept them away." Three situations where God alone won the battle are the template for the present petition.
10: נִשְׁמְדוּ בְעֵין-דֹּאר (nishĕmĕdû bĕʿên-dōʾr, "they were destroyed at En-dor!") — shāmad (niphal: to be annihilated). En-dor: near the Jezreel valley at the foot of Mount Moreh, near Gideon's battlefield (not the En-dor of the witch — 1 Samuel 28:7). הָיוּ דֹמֶן לָאֲדָמָה (hāyû dōmen lāʾădāmāh, "they became dung for the ground!") — dōmen: dung, manure (Jeremiah 8:2; Psalms 9:22 — "they shall be like dung on the surface of the ground"). The bodies of the powerful coalition become fertilizer — the ultimate reversal of their ambition to wipe Israel from the earth.
11: שִׁיתֵמוֹ נְדִיבֵימוֹ כְּעֹרֵב וְכִזְאֵב (shîtêmô nĕdîbêmô kĕʿōrêb wĕkhizʾêb, "make their nobles like Oreb and Zeeb!") — Oreb (Raven) and Zeeb (Wolf): the two princes of Midian captured and executed by the Ephraimites at the Jordan during Gideon's pursuit — Judges 7:25 ("they killed Oreb at the rock of Oreb, and Zeeb at the wine press of Zeeb"). וְכִזְבַח וּכְצַלְמֻנָּע כָּל-נְסִיכֵימוֹ (wĕkhizbaḥ ûkĕṣalmunnāʿ kol-nĕsîkêmô, "and all their princes like Zebah and Zalmunna!") — the two kings of Midian whom Gideon personally executed as blood-avengers for his brothers — Judges 8:5-21. All four Midianite leaders are paradigms of God's power to eliminate the most dangerous enemies.
12: אֲשֶׁר אָמְרוּ נִירְשָׁה לָּנוּ אֵת נְאוֹת אֱלֹהִים (ʾăsher ʾāmĕrû nîrĕshāh llānû ʾêt nĕʾôt ʾĕlōhîm, "who said, 'Let us seize for ourselves the pastures of God!'") — nāʾāh: pastureland, the pleasant meadows (Psalms 23:2: "green pastures"). Nîrĕshāh: we will inherit/possess (from yārash: the covenantal inheritance term). The coalition's specific goal: not just to defeat Israel but to take possession of the land promised to Abraham. God's land claimed by those who reject his covenant (Leviticus 25:23: "the land is mine").
13: אֱלֹהַי שִׁיתֵמוֹ כַגַּלְגַּל כְּקַשׁ לִפְנֵי-רוּחַ (ʾĕlōhay shîtêmô khagalgal kĕqash lipnê-rûaḥ, "O my God, make them like whirling dust, like chaff before the wind!") — galgal: a wheel, something rolling — specifically the round, hollow-stemmed tumbleweed that breaks off at the root in autumn and rolls across the landscape driven by wind (Isaiah 17:13: "like rolling things before the wind"; Job 21:18). Qash: dry straw/chaff (the winnowed lightweight residue — Psalms 1:4; Isaiah 40:24). The coalition that threatened permanent erasure of Israel is itself to be erased without trace.
14: כְּאֵשׁ תִּבְעַר-יָעַר וּכְלַהֲבָה תְּלַהֵט הָרִים (kĕʾêsh tibʿar-yāʿar ûkĕlahăbāh tĕlahêṭ hārîm, "as fire consumes the forest, as the flame sets the mountains ablaze!") — two fire images for thoroughness: bāʿar (to burn/consume — the complete combustion that does not stop until everything combustible is gone); lāhaṭ (piel: to flame fiercely, to scorch — the setting-ablaze of the mountains). The fire imagery prepares for v15's petition: "pursue them with your storm and terrify them with your tempest."
15: כֵּן תִּרְדְּפֵם בְּסַעֲרֶךָ (kên tirdĕpêm bĕsaʿărekā, "so pursue them with your storm!") — sāʿar: the violent storm-wind (the whirlwind that took Elijah — 2 Kings 2:1; YHWH's answer to Job — Job 38:1). וּבְסוּפָתְךָ תְבַהֲלֵם (ûbĕsûpātĕkā tĕbahălêm, "and terrify them with your tempest!") — sûpāh: a tornado (Proverbs 1:27; Isaiah 17:13). Bāhal (piel: to terrify, to throw into sudden panic — Joshua 10:10; 1 Samuel 7:10). The petition connects to Sisera's precedent: the Kishon river storm panicked his chariots (Judges 5:20-21: "the stars fought from heaven... against Sisera").
16: מַלֵּא פְנֵיהֶם קָלוֹן (mallêʾ pĕnêhem qālôn, "fill their faces with shame!") — qālôn: dishonor, disgrace (the public opposite of kābôd/glory — Proverbs 3:35). וִיבַקְשׁוּ שִׁמְךָ יְהוָה (wîbaqshû shimĕkā yĕhwāh, "that they may seek your name, O LORD!") — the pivotal purpose clause: the shame of v16 has a redemptive goal. The enemies are shamed so that they will seek the divine name. Bāqash shêm: to seek the name (acknowledging and pursuing the God who has acted — Isaiah 55:6: "seek the LORD while he may be found"). The imprecation has a missionary ambition: judgment as the path to the enemies' conversion.
17: יֵבֹשׁוּ וְיִבָּהֲלוּ עֲדֵי-עַד (yēbōshû wĕyibbāhălû ʿădê-ʿad, "let them be put to shame and dismayed forever!") — bôsh: to be put to shame; bāhal (niphal: to be terrified, thrown into disarray). ʿĂdê-ʿad: "forever, unto eternity" — the temporal absolute for those who do not repent. וְיַחְפְּרוּ וְיֹאבֵדוּ (wĕyaḥpĕrû wĕyōʾbêdû, "let them be disgraced and perish!") — ḥāpar: to be confounded (to dig/scratch in the dust in humiliation); ʾābed: to perish. This verse covers those who refuse the redemptive shame of vv16,18 — the two tracks: shame-leading-to-seeking or shame-leading-to-perishing.
18: וְיֵדְעוּ כִּי-אַתָּה שִׁמְךָ יְהוָה לְבַדֶּךָ עֶלְיוֹן עַל-כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (wĕyêdĕʿû kî-ʾattāh shimĕkā yĕhwāh lĕbaddĕkā ʿelyôn ʿal-kol-hāʾāreṣ, "let them know that you alone, whose name is the LORD, are the Most High over all the earth!") — the goal of the entire imprecatory psalm: not Israel's vengeance but universal knowledge (yādaʿ) of who YHWH is. Lĕbaddĕkā: "you alone" (Deuteronomy 32:12). ʿElyôn: the Most High (Genesis 14:18-22; Daniel 7:18,22). The psalm that began with the coalition plotting to erase Israel's name ends with the petition that the nations know the divine name — the eschatological reversal of Ps 2: conspiracy against God ends in the nations' recognition of his sovereignty.
